frameworks.name: Logic-Evidence-Utility Problem Definition
frameworks.definition: A three-question framework for defining business problems: What does it mean? How do you know that? Why do you care? Forces quantitative grounding of vague claims.

frameworks.name: Fast Cash Play via Existing Resources
frameworks.definition: A structured approach to generating immediate client revenue by mobilizing the client's existing dead contacts, old customers, or social media audience before new acquisition begins, making the product self-funding within 30 days.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a consulting business has more inbound demand than it can convert, the real constraint is usually not lead volume but the qualification and indoctrination of prospects before a sales call — specifically, prospects arrive without knowing what the product is, making every sales call an education session instead of a close.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: organic traffic as primary lead source
claims.mechanism: Without pre-call indoctrination, prospects arrive with mismatched expectations, requiring the salesperson to bridge the gap between what they imagined and what is actually offered before any close attempt is possible, consuming call time and lowering conversion.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that adding a simple pre-qualifying checkbox (e.g., 'I want a VIP ticket') to an opt-in page produced a 16% increase in sales of the corresponding upsell on the next page, because the checkbox functions as a pre-commitment device that primes the prospect to accept the subsequent offer.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: opt-in funnel with a multi-step page sequence
upsell offer present on a downstream page
claims.mechanism: Checking a box expressing intent creates a micro-commitment that is cognitively consistent with then purchasing the corresponding product on the next page, reducing resistance at the point of offer.

numbers.axis: sales conversion lift from pre-commitment checkbox

bounded_by: Checkbox produced no change to the subsequent page or process — the lift came purely from the pre-framing effect

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that for businesses with large inbound traffic volumes, inserting a video sales letter (VSL) before a sales call functions as a filtering mechanism that qualifies in high-fit prospects and removes low-fit ones, increasing the proportion of closeable prospects on the calendar.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: organic content as primary traffic source
claims.mechanism: A VSL sets explicit expectations about what the product is and who it is for, so only prospects who have self-selected through that filter book calls, making each call more likely to be closeable.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that adding qualification friction to an inbound funnel increases cost per call while increasing ROAS by a larger multiple, and simultaneously doubles or triples sales team efficiency — meaning the same revenue can be achieved with a smaller team and less operational drag.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Higher qualification friction filters out low-intent prospects before a call is booked; the remaining prospects have higher purchase probability, so each call produces more revenue even though fewer calls are booked.

numbers.axis: cost per call vs. ROAS under increasing qualification friction

bounded_by: Bad friction (slow pages, hard-to-find forms) turns off all prospects regardless of fit — only good friction (explicit qualification gates) creates the separation

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the questions asked on an application or pre-call form directly increase the probability that a prospect purchases when the offer is made, because those questions function as a pre-commitment and reverse-engineer the sale before the call begins.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Each question a prospect answers about their problem and goals constitutes a micro-commitment to the problem state that the offer solves; by the time the call starts they have already articulated why they need the solution.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that the fastest cash-generation plays for consulting or service businesses operate from existing resources — specifically dead contacts, lapsed customers, or an existing social media following — rather than requiring new lead generation.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: existing social media presence or client list
claims.mechanism: Existing contacts have prior exposure to the business and require less trust-building than cold prospects, making re-engagement campaigns faster and cheaper to convert into revenue.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that his operating principle for any client engagement is that the client should have made back the cost of the program within the first 30 days, by deploying a fast-cash campaign as the first onboarding step — making the engagement effectively self-funding from found money.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: client has an existing customer list or social audience
claims.mechanism: A campaign targeting existing underutilized contacts generates revenue quickly at near-zero acquisition cost; this cash offsets the program fee, removing the net cost objection and accelerating client commitment.

numbers.axis: time for client to recoup program investment

anchors.value: fast cash campaign on existing contacts in onboarding week 1

bounded_by: Requires client to have an existing list, customer base, or social following large enough to generate meaningful re-engagement revenue

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that when a consulting client fails to take action despite receiving a blueprint and one-on-one support, the solution is structural incentive design within the offer — such as bonuses or discounts unlocked by completing specific activation actions, or guarantees contingent on the client taking defined steps — rather than relying on intrinsic client motivation.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: client inaction is primary churn cause
claims.mechanism: Tying bonuses, discounts, or guarantees to specific early actions creates an external incentive structure that substitutes for intrinsic motivation; clients who would not self-activate will act to capture the conditional reward.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that a business owner who is not allocating attention and resources to their highest-return activities is the fundamental reason their business does not grow — and that a new operator taking over and reallocating those same resources typically produces immediate growth from identical inputs.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Founders develop blind spots to underutilized resources (contacts, followers, goodwill) that a fresh operator sees as obvious; the growth comes from resource reallocation, not resource addition.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that vague problem statements like 'our messaging is off' must be forced through three diagnostic questions — what does it mean (definition), how do you know (evidence), and why do I care (utility) — before any tactical solution is worth pursuing, because without this forcing function, teams optimize the wrong variable.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: team or founder troubleshooting growth constraint
claims.mechanism: Vague labels like 'messaging' cover multiple distinct failure modes (unqualified prospects, wrong offer framing, sales team skill gap) that require different fixes; without precise definition and evidence, effort is allocated to the symptom rather than the cause.

examples.text: During the session, Alex Hormozi's team ran a book launch opt-in where a checkbox appeared asking if the registrant wanted a VIP ticket. The checkbox triggered no change in the subsequent page or process. Despite this, simply checking the box made prospects more likely to purchase the VIP upsell when it was presented on the next page. Hormozi used this to illustrate how pre-framing questions shape purchase behavior: checking the box established an intent signal that made the subsequent purchase feel consistent. The measured result was a 16% lift in VIP sales attributable solely to the checkbox opt-in.

Alex Hormozi described the restaurant operations consultant's situation as a paradigm case of misdiagnosed demand. The founder said 'my messaging is off' as the explanation for not hitting $10M. Hormozi pressed: what does 'messaging off' mean? The founder eventually revealed that prospects were booking calls without knowing what the product was — arriving curious rather than qualified. This meant the sales call began with education rather than with a close attempt. Hormozi used this example to demonstrate that 'messaging is off' is not one problem but a bundle — in this case, a pre-call indoctrination gap that required a VSL funnel fix, not a messaging copy fix.

Hormozi illustrated the fast cash play concept by describing its application to service consultants onboarding new clients: the first deliverable in onboarding is a campaign targeting the client's existing dead contacts, old customers, or social followers. This campaign is designed to generate revenue for the client within the first 30 days — enough to cover the cost of the program. Hormozi noted this is how he has operated throughout his career: he never wants someone to pay him money he has not already made them. The restaurant consultant's situation was cited as a direct fit: the operator's clients are restaurants that have lapsed relationships with former customers, which a re-engagement campaign could monetize quickly at near-zero acquisition cost.

practices.text: Include a VSL (video sales letter) or pre-call information page before any inbound sales call booking to educate prospects on exactly what you offer and who it is for, so they arrive at the call already informed.

Design application or opt-in questions to reverse-engineer the sale: ask prospects about their problem, what they have tried, and what outcome they want, so they have articulated the need before the call begins.

Tie any guarantee or bonus in a consulting program to the client completing specific activation actions (e.g., 3 defined steps) rather than making it unconditional, to create enforceable behavioral incentives.

Test the fast cash play with a small beta cohort before rolling it into standard onboarding to validate that the re-engagement campaign generates sufficient revenue to cover the program cost within 30 days.

When diagnosing any growth constraint, force the team through three questions in sequence: what does this term actually mean (define it operationally), how do you know it is the problem (quantitative evidence), and why does solving it matter to revenue (utility test).
